I'll start from the very beginning of my Rails career, which was at an agency-slash-consulting firm, local in Monterey, sort of like a boutique sort of situation, not very huge.
I started as an individual contributor there as a Rails-like primarily backend developer.
And I worked my way through the ranks really quickly at that company and became sort of like a CTO-slash-COO of that boutique agency in a matter of like two and a half years, something like that.
Yeah, and that was pretty fast.
But what happened there is like, honestly, the bar, the technical bar in Mexico is like slower, to be quite honest.
So it just like, Rails just came to me naturally.
And so I was very appreciated at the company like really quickly and became like...
Very instrumental to the company.
So that's what happened there.
I, at some point in that journey, realized like, you know what, I could, I think I have the chops to make it internationally.
And that's like a whole different market, right?
So that was very appealing to me, like salary wise.

Fernandez.

Very early stage, like they had just raised seed funding when I joined and sort of got that green field experience, right?
Like brand new product, brand new ideas.
And I was fortunate to actually completely pivot the product while I was there.
So I got to build like the foundation of their current offering, which is doing very well.
I'm happy to say, yeah.

Their CTO is –

I enjoyed working with him as well.
And then I made the jump to 37signals, which...
So you were at Magma for like six months.
What made you want to jump so quick?
Okay.
So Magma, what happened there is I wanted...
I saw the reality that consulting work always has to have a margin, you know?
And then Zipline made it very evident that they were willing to like hire me directly.
And I'm like, why am I going through an intermediary and giving them a part of a cake, if you know what I mean?
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
So I started conversations with Zipline and then Arrows reached out because they had read some of my posts online.
And it just happened that I chose to go with Arrows, but that's what it was.
Yeah, so like they were doing consulting, enterprise consulting with like folks allocated, basically dedicated to that client, right?
Exactly, exactly.
And they were paying like wages that were good in Mexico, but like not worldwide, right?
So when I realized like I could be hired internationally, so I figured why not do that?
Yeah, that's very similar to the experience that I had like in Brazil.

like I had a small dev shop, me and four other guys, and then eventually we got acquired by a bigger one called CodeMiner that has a bunch of like the, it was like 50 people or 60 people at the time, and it was the same place.
It's just like you're using folks that are like still early in their career, and maybe they have like a language barrier, and you're like make a margin on that.
And then you have the whole, the whole dynamic of sometimes you put like super senior people first, and then you flip to more junior.
Once it's allocated, it's not, it shouldn't exist in my mind.
At least tech companies should have their teams internally.
But yeah, I was just curious, because so many folks who do consulting and they love it, and I was completely the opposite for most of the time.

Cool, so like arrows, did you guys, so during all this time, like I was kind of like in this different setup where like 2013, React was coming up, and then I started doing some stuff at it, and then I switched.
I took over Trevor's code base from another company and Jonas, and it was all vanilla Rails.
So I didn't get to like touch React for like six, seven years, but the whole market was already like doing like Rails and sometimes React in the front.
So was it arrows already?
Because I know they are big on React now, right?
Right, that's a good question, actually.
What happened there is that the app is hotwire, but when I mentioned the pivot, what we did with that pivot was very deeply integrate with HubSpot specifically.
So previously we would integrate with like tons of CRMs.
After the pivot, was just HubSpot.
And they have like a React SDK thing where you can like embed your app into their own, right, into HubSpot.
So that's why they're big on React.
Last I talked about that with Matt, they're like, they're still doing both.
But yeah.

Yeah, and then like you joined 37 and like were you on the prod, were you on the hey team or were on the base team?
Because they had the split at the time, right?
So yeah, so I was primarily assigned to base camp.
I was.
The way 37signals works is kind of, it's really cool.
I really like that part of the job.
It's essentially one or two programmers, tops, per product.
So I was, when I was there, like two and a half years, I was the Basecamp guy.
Like no one else, people came and went, but I was like the Basecamp programmer.
That's crazy.
Did they still do that?
It's like every, oh man.
And then like I did, like we did on-call rotations every eight-ish weeks, something like that.
Eight or seven weeks.
And when you're on call, you touch every single product.
So I did get exposure to like every single thing through that Hay and also like the legacy products, like Campfire, the original Campfire.
Right, Yeah.
And also got to work, towards the end, I was unassigned from Basecamp and assigned to what is now a publicly known cup.
Coming product, which is Fizzy.
So evergreen, like new product stuff.
It was fun.
I did that for like four months, something like that before leaving.
Well, how many people, how many people on the product team are?
What are they doing?
There was one person on Basecamp.
What are everyone else doing?
Right.

So there's like, there were like two or three assigned to Hey, and that's, it's different with Basecamp because it's like, it's been around or, you know, so it wasn't, I think it sort of like depends on what Jason and David want to work on.
And when they have tons of ideas for Basecamp, like they'll assign more people.
But usually what I saw is like Basecamp got a steady flow of like upcoming of like new features, but like not enough to justify like a whole team.
was just like, it was just Jose and a designer and like, we would push that.
But the whole, like the bulk of the new ideas were going into Hey.
They might have gone into new products.
They've started working on products and then scrapped them.
That happened.
So people were assigned to those and then not.
And yeah, just basically that.
And then the team isn't really big at all, right?
I forget how many programmers there were.
want to say like 10, maybe 10 product programmers.
And then there's like ops folks, which they deal with the databases and whatnot, but servers.
It's always wild to me that they always have like almost the same amount of people doing ops and people doing product.
Yeah.
Yeah, that's cool.
And like, yeah, when I was like, I've talked to David many times, but he was big on the idea of like creating a new code base for every new version.
So like when I was creating a new version of when I was doing the pivot from the original, you recover.
And to know your team, he's like, you guys should create a new product and just keep the other one running and don't do anything else on the other code base.
So, like, it's very easy to scrap products if you do like that.
And we ended up not doing it.
I don't regret not doing that because it ended up being, like, an evolution of another company anyway.
But, yeah, so that's interesting.
And they also always have, like, the rewrites, right?
So, like, we have Basecamp 2, Basecamp 3, that's usually separate.
Yeah, they are different code bases.
And I'll say one thing I learned about that approach is that they do treat the new version as a completely new product.
And that's, like, part of the reason.
It's, like, they'll start, they'll literally start from scratch, like, questioning every single thing about what they previously knew.
And, of course, coming at it a second or third time, you know that much more about that domain model.
And so, Basecamp is just a beautiful code base.
Because of that, right?
Because we had Basecamp 1 and 2 before.
And so the domain model is just perfectly suited to what it does.
And I know for Basecamp 4 and 5, 5 is what they're working on currently.
I think they've tweeted about it once or twice.
Yeah, Basecamp essentially for the two years that you were there and like some newer product at the end.
Like what were some of the challenges that you have like working on?
Like I kind of, I got...
An experience of like working on the Basecamp kind of came, I think Basecamp 3 came off of a lot of stuff that Jonas and Trevor were doing with Honcho that they renamed to another company.
So I have, and I've seen the code base of Basecamp 3 as well a couple of times by pairing with Trevor when he was still there.
Like what were the, the, of the challenges that you guys, that you're dealing with, like it wasn't just building new features or like maintaining the thing, the number of users or.
Yeah, that's a good question.
Millions of users.
Yeah, exactly.
I think that's like a multi, I can give a multifaceted answer because day to day, I think I can think of two things that were always top of mind.
And one is yes, the scale for sure, like millions of users, hundreds of thousands of requests for, I'm going to say minutes.
But sometimes second, and depending on which endpoint.
And so like the scale for sure, you need to consider every single thing.
It's always funny to me when people rediscover database indexes in Twitter, and they're like, I just created an index and it's so much faster.
I'm like, dude, that's like table stakes.
You can't deploy a feature without an index, especially working in something like Basecamp.
So yes, and I came up also with database migrations, and you need to be like very thoughtful of how to go about those because of the throughput.
But also the second thing that comes to mind is code quality.
Like I've never worked at somewhere where that is number one priority for programmers.
I became so much better at programming.
Like I learned a ton.
But even like even the most experienced programmers on the team will tell you like Rosa, Jeff, they'll steal the guy like there.
They worry about that because David will come in, in the PR and be like, he'll find something regardless of who's working on it.
Even Jeremy, Jeff, Rosa, Jorge, all these like big names.
And of course myself, like we're all thinking about the code quality and that can sometimes slow you down a little bit.
So I found out like when working at Harvest, I needed to tone it down a little bit so I can move a little bit faster.
So, but in, in the end, like my code is that much better for that experience, you know?
Yeah, those are two things that come to mind.
And then there are other like technical stuff that we could go into about like what's hard in Basecamp specifically, but that's like, that would be in the weeds, but we can talk about it if you want.
Yeah, that would be cool.
But like, I'm always like nerdy, I like nerdy about this stuff.
But yeah, in my mind, like, especially dealing with Rails, like the nature of the thing being a monolith, it does make a lot of sense.
It's to worry about organization and code quality, or at least shielding things so they don't leak everywhere.
But yeah, to a certain extent, like one thing that in my mind, like there's, at least like, because I had David and Jason as board advisors for another company for so long.
And it was, we always had a little bit of like, you guys are alive, you know, like, and you guys are not in survival mode.
So you can get to move a little slower, or you get all the, you are an unicorn in itself.
It's very hard to reproduce the stability you have and the pace that you can have, where other places you're like, ah, better move.
Absolutely.
Yeah, for sure.
That's a big thing.
That's one of the things that allows them to like, scrap products, again, like we talked about, like they can do that.
Because business is healthy, they're profitable, they can, they can allow themselves time.
I'm to explore ideas and be like, nah, you know?
Yeah, yeah.
That's a luxury.
There's a few big, small companies, small, quote unquote, that can do that.
Exactly, yep.
Cool.
How was the recording system that you guys had?
That's one part of the code base I was always like, how does this thing work?
And I can remember Trevor was there in the beginning and we were talking about it.
Yeah, recordings are great.
I was going to say pattern, but the recording itself is not the pattern.
It's just polymorphism taken to a very extreme, not extreme, but very advanced level.
And that is actually what I was thinking about when I was alluding to what's difficult in Basecamp.
Recordings enable so much behavior sharing, and that's great.
That is super power.
Like, you can, when we implemented new features in.
Basecamp, which is rare, but we did do that.
Every now and then we would create a new type of recording.

It was so easy to just like plug into the existing system and immediately you already have notifications, have threaded comments, you have like every single thing that comes with any other tool in Basecamp.
What's difficult about it sometimes is that they create like this nested architecture that is sometimes like it can get a little bit deep.
And so you need to be smart about like, oh, how deep can this hierarchy go?
Because if I'm going to be querying this in the database, like if you, it's essentially like a branch system, like there's a trunk and there are branches and there are leaf nodes.
If you want to go from the leaf to the trunk, that is very, not hard, but consuming resource intensive when you have billions of database records, right?
But if you go from the trunk outwards, then it's lightning fast.
Um...
So yeah, just something that you need to think about.
Yeah, that's interesting because I remember there's so much normalization in this.
Like this must be a nightmare for maintenance, but it was an interesting idea.
Pros and cons with anything, know, trade-offs, but it did enable us to move very quickly when implementing new stuff for sure.
And also, this was one thing that I wanted to mention when you were talking about like monolith.
But it is a good way to enable maintainers to keep the whole system in their minds.
Yeah, because like a single person can feasibly understand Basecamp as a whole.
And partly because there aren't that many models because they're all recordings.
David needs to, he's alluded to wanting to, yeah, he's been meaning to like record a video on that pattern.
And he really needs to get into it because it's a great, it's a great pattern.
It gets to go.
I don't know if you can tell, but I'm getting excited.
Yeah, that was one of the things that Trevor was like, oh, David just put this  recording things in the project.
I was getting fed up by that.
And then it was just the beginning.
So he wasn't sure if it was going to work.
And I remember looking at it, too.
But cool.
then, so you, like, yeah, it's like the Vanilla Rails experience is super nice.
Like, that's one thing that I was so torn about when, like, I switched it to, to GrowthX.
And I was toying with a bunch of different stuff in between and, like, building little apps there.
Like, the React ecosystem, at least to me, is, like, so mature that at least the UI side of things, you get it done so fast.
While, like, at Basecamp, it made sense because, like, you always, they always had, like, design.
Like, it was, I don't know if you guys continue to do that, but at least know your company for Base.
There's almost no sharing.
Like, there's, like, like.
By design, everything was scoped by almost like the file, the viewer, like the feature itself, and not a lot of thinking to like componentization or design systems and stuff like that.
And that is nice because you change a lot of stuff without thinking about breaking other places.
But when you get something like ChedCN and you get something like Claude trained in ChedCN, you get people like back-end programmers building like beautifully looking things.
It's like, okay, that's a different level of productivity.
So I've made the jump to like use React.
I don't love it, but I also don't want to go back.
I don't know if you, like if you, like I think of Harvest is React, right?
Like almost everything that's not Basecamp is like Moostag, like it's this split.
Or React somewhere.
Yeah, I know what you mean.
I think, like, we used React at Harvest.
And I think it has its place.
Like you said, like, your considerations for using React, I think, are sound.

I think going back to the luxury of being a thriving company that can have, like, the luxury of taking your time with things, I think the vanilla Rails approach is really well suited for that because they're not going to use ChatCN components, right?
They're going to want to build their own.
And so you have the luxury of starting from scratch and just building the thing yourself.
If you want to go fast, I do think it makes sense to use any number of React libraries, component libraries, for sure.
Price to pay in terms of...
Of build complexity, for sure.
And then sometimes just like fighting the impulse to have too much state on the front end, you know?
Yeah.
Because React has so much tooling for that.
You're like, should I do this on the client side?
It requires good judgment on the part of the implementer, which is very rare, unfortunately.
Yeah, I agree.
Like we were essentially using the inertia setup where like every controller has its own screen and we only like fall back to like full React when we really need.
So there's a couple of places in the product where we use state management and everything.
And that's like, it's the worst part of the code base, but it's so live.
Like there's like things bubbling up in real time.
Like a text editor with an AI editor on the side and like that stuff, it would be a nightmare to do.
you.
Thank
Total streams, but if it was not in this setup, but cool, man, like that makes a lot of sense.
How was the transition from working from a pretty themed, super nice code base, and I assume Harvest is a much bigger company with probably a very old code base as well.
They've been around.
You know, it was interesting for sure because, to be completely honest, I'll put it this way, try to be very diplomatic about it.
Harvest wasn't nearly as rigorous engineering wise.
So even with, it's not about the legacy code.
It's not about the React in the front end.
It's just like you can immediately see some questionable decisions, which trade-offs, you know.
You can only assume they had to go fast for whatever reason.
They're obviously very capable and intelligent programmers, but they didn't spend as much time thinking about the maintainability of it.
And you do spend more time reading code than you do writing it.
So I think there's merit to thinking and caring about that.
One thing I'm very thankful for in my career as a programmer is having gone through that 37signals phase, my baseline implementations are usually pretty good, if I might say so myself.
So I'm able to, even if I don't painstakingly polish everything the same way I would if DHH was going to review my PR, even when I don't do that, like the, I can still go pretty fast.
And also like the code is pretty, it's clean enough.
Yeah.
It's like, it's, it's ship.
It's definitely shippable.
So more than shippable.
It's like, it's me.

Containable, it's very clean, maybe not like DHH grade polish, but if I, again, like trade-offs, like if I spent another day or two on this, I could get it that way, or we can just ship and start the next feature, especially at some place like GrowthX where we're getting started, right?
We need to go quickly.
Yeah, that's funny, because like I was, I was actually like, I was listening to David's next interview a week after we talked, and then I forgot how picky he is about all these things.

Oh yeah, like, and I'm thinking like, I don't know, that's nice, like thinking about code poetry and everything, that sounds great, like it makes a lot of sense in your setup, and I think Fathom, like we need to do the feature like every couple of weeks here.
Yeah, so that's a completely different reality, but, so like, that's my whole, my whole approach to recruiting everybody was...
Get folks that are super experienced and tell them, like, don't worry about it.
Your baseline is going to be good enough, probably.
So you don't even have to, like, there's no requirement for mandatory PRs.
If you need a review or if you think you're going to benefit, ask that in the beginning when we can still change the architecture.
And then if you need a second pair of eyes, then ask at the end for review.
The reviews are not mandatory because everybody here can probably hold a baseline to stay gainable enough.
And then we revisit.
And, like, my hope is that we have the sandbox where GrowthX is the tool for GrowthX.
We were actually just discussing this yesterday.
What are we going to do?
Big picture of the company.
And I don't know if you, Marcel, covered that.
But we have, and I can walk you through that later if you want.
But the plan is to have GrowthX be a services business.
It's with our own internal tool that most of the clients were not going to interact with that.
I think you probably spent time with Brad figuring out the magic link and all that stuff because we don't want to give people access to the whole thing because we're doing too many things.
We're testing too many things.
Maybe the UI is janky.
Maybe the feature idea is not sound.
But out of that, we want to start extracting the stuff that we want to give access to other folks.
And then that part extracted should have the same level of care that any self-serving product would have.
And so ideally, it would be that kind of similar to Basecamp where they have the opportunity to try things out, like Honcho, and then some things came back into Basecamp and Honcho spun off.
Or every couple of years, you get a chance to do a rewrite because it's a new version.
So in our case, ideally, would be like the sandboxes where we try everything for ourselves.
Then we spin out the stuff that we know are useful to the whole team.
So that's how we're approaching things.
So I'm less concerned about the big monolith that we're building super fast for ourselves and more concerned about the little stuff that we're going to pull out.
And that will require a little bit more attention.
But still, it's going to be like smaller teams on this and will be like the same team shared.
And then you have the framework team that's trying to figure out our infrastructure for AI workflows.
And I don't know if I don't, my memory sucks now.
I don't know, I've been meeting so many people.
I don't know if I've showed you the whole thing, but.
Yeah, we went through it.
Yeah.
So that's kind of, that makes sense.
So, cool.
What are the, what are the things that, a couple of questions that I, that I've always like asking everybody, like what's, what are the things?
are the
So the first one is like, how are you feeling about the whole impact of AI-assisted coding or coding agents and things like CloudCode, Cursor, Augment, and all that stuff, both lovable.
There's a whole spectrum of different tools.
Yeah.
Good question.
I'm for them.
Like, I'm optimistic about them.
I do use them.

I've found, especially at the very beginning of a project, if the ambition is to build something that is flexible and scalable, I have had a very hard time getting them, meaning the LLMs, to come up with something that is, yeah, the design of the architecture that's worthwhile.
So what I've done usually...
And I have this, I'll use this as an example.
have this project that I'm toying with that very feasibly could become a side business at some point.
But it's AI tools for YouTube creators.
So things like creating your thumbnail, but also like A-B testing them, putting tons of, yeah.
And then titles and then comments, sentiment analysis on the comments and whatnot.
So things like that.
If you were to join, we should definitely help you launch that.
Yeah, we could do that, of course.
And then, so to use as an example, it was important for that product to be able to use the same infrastructure and workflows for creating like tons of different media, right?
From thumbnails to titles to scripts to like videos potentially.
But at the end, it's all like, it's an AI pipeline, right?
Yeah.
So they just couldn't do it.
Like, whatever I threw at it, like, it came back.
And I'm like, this is not going to be flexible enough that it can produce any type of media.
It would, like, narrow down on thumbnails or videos.
So I started with, like, the flexible, polymorphic, robust architecture.
And once I had that, I had it, I had cloud code go into my code and document it, or rather, like, extract guidelines from it.
And about how I used polymorphism and how I designed the system, put it in a markdown file.
And then, only then, was it able to, like, go into that, use that as context, and provide something that is 80% of the way there for, like, a principle-level code base.
For junior stuff, it's great.
But if you're going to do something that you want to, like, put on the market and scale reasonably well, then it has some heart.
It has a hard time with that, is what I do.
Yeah, yeah.
No, I agree.
And I don't know if I showed you much of how we create the workflows, but essentially, the beginning was, like, Marcel built all this stuff in AeroOps.
My next step was, like, okay, I need to migrate 100-plus workflows out of this tool into code base, because they were getting complicated, and I wanted to make it more complicated.

So I ended up creating, like, a set of conventions on how to create AI workflows, and came up with the folder structure, and I spent the bulk of the work just, like, documenting everything, writing a ton of readme's, and, like, structuring how AI could work with that.
So Cursor, at the time, was using Cursor, so Cursor could automatically load the folder, and all the supporting documents, and all the rules, and generate the workflows 80% there.
But it took, like, took a month to figure out what is the right runtime.
What's the folder structure and all that.
Once that was done, then it's like, no, it's pretty fast.
And we're trying to figure out to build an agent to do that even better.
So we run the tests, try the workflow, see if it gets the output right.
